name: Cherry Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Cherry. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Cherry surface cleaning without thermal damage

wavelength.description: Near-infrared wavelength for optimal absorption in Cherry organic compounds

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning control

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for continuous cleaning process

energyDensity.description: Fluence threshold for effective contaminant removal

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for efficient ablation of contaminants

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for efficient area coverage

passCount.description: Number of cleaning passes for complete surface treatment

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning coverage

dwellTime.description: Effective dwell time per spot for contaminant removal

power.description: Typical range for wood cleaning at 1064 nm to remove contaminants without charring; starting at 50 W for delicate hardwoods like cherry to minimize thermal damage. Max safe ~200 W to avoid substrate ablation. Source: Studies on laser cleaning of wood (e.g., Journal of Cultural Heritage, 2018); IPG Photonics YLR series specs for cleaning applications.

scan_speed.description: Effective speeds for contaminant removal on wood; 500 mm/s recommended for optimal balance of cleaning efficiency and heat control on cherry wood. Slower (100-500 mm/s) for heavy contamination, faster (1000-2000 mm/s) for light. Source: Industry standards from Laser Cleaning Europe guidelines and equipment manuals (e.g., Adapt Laser Systems).

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for wood to ablate contaminants with minimal heat-affected zone; 20 ns recommended for cherry to reduce thermal diffusion given wood's low conductivity (~0.16 W/m·K). Picosecond (ps) or femtosecond optional for ultra-precision but less common industrially. Source: Laser physics research in Optics and Lasers in Engineering (2020).

repetition_rate.description: Typical for pulsed cleaning of organics; 30 kHz balances speed and heat accumulation on cherry wood (reflectivity ~40% at 1064 nm, melting point N/A but charring >200°C). Higher rates increase speed but risk buildup. Source: Academic literature, e.g., Applied Surface Science (2019) on wood laser processing.

energy_density.description: Fluence range for safe contaminant removal; cherry wood damage threshold ~8-12 J/cm² at 1064 nm due to low thermal conductivity. 1.5 J/cm² recommended for cleaning without substrate harm. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Journal of Applied Physics (2017) for hardwoods.

spot_size.description: Beam diameter for precise cleaning; 200 μm recommended for cherry surface preparation to cover details without overexposure. Smaller for fine work, larger for broad areas. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent and Trumpf laser cleaning systems.

pass_count.description: Multi-pass for thorough removal on wood; 2 passes recommended for cherry to ensure complete contamination clearance while monitoring for discoloration. Single pass for light soils. Source: Industry case studies in restoration (e.g., ICALEO proceedings, 2021).

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform coverage; 60% recommended for cherry to avoid gaps or excessive overlap heating, given wood's sensitivity. Source: Process optimization in Laser & Photonics Reviews (2016).
